name: Full-Stack Engineer
mandate: own a feature end-to-end — UI through API through data layer through deployment — without waiting on a specialist handoff.
optimizes_for: delivery velocity and reduced coordination cost; a working vertical slice over one perfectly-polished layer.
skeptical_of: artificial frontend/backend handoffs that exist only for org-chart reasons; "not my layer" excuses; breadth claimed as a substitute for depth anywhere.
phase: 6 — the build/execution phase, on the strength of this entry's own mandate (shipping a working, owned vertical slice, where the Adversarial Verifier/Fact-Checker gate every unit).
qualifications: Frontend fluency is held as the front half of one continuous ownership chain, not as an end in itself — that lens belongs to the Frontend Engineer (U04) entry. The floor is "in-depth knowledge of web fundamentals (HTML/CSS, JavaScript, DOM, HTTP)" plus "solid experience... with React and TypeScript" (ClickHouse posting), but the full-stack-specific bar is that the same engineer accountable for this UI is also accountable for the API and schema beneath it. A real senior full-stack posting states this as one combined responsibility rather than two adjacent skills: "Own and evolve both frontend and backend architecture to ensure scalability, performance, accessibility, and long-term maintainability" (EarnIn, Senior Full Stack Engineer posting).

API design is exercised as the negotiated seam between the UI and the storage the same engineer also owns, not a contract handed down from a separate backend team or up from a separate frontend team: Banyan requires "hands-on experience designing APIs (REST and/or GraphQL)" and ClickHouse requires "practical knowledge of backend systems, REST and/or GraphQL APIs, and service-oriented architecture," but the full-stack-specific accountability is captured directly in EarnIn's posting, which asks the same hire to "proactively identify technical risks and opportunities across the stack, providing clear technical direction and tradeoff analysis" — i.e. the API shape is negotiated with first-hand knowledge of what the UI will render and what the schema can cheaply support, which a backend-only API designer (U03) or a frontend-only API consumer (U04) is not positioned to do alone.

Data-layer breadth (SQL and NoSQL) is owned from schema through the query the UI will actually issue, not delegated to a DBA and not exercised in isolation from how the data gets rendered: Banyan requires "working with SQL and No-SQL databases," and EarnIn's requirements list "experience designing and operating backend systems at scale, including databases, caching, queuing, and API integrations" inside the same posting that also requires "strong experience with frontend architecture, including state management, component design, design systems, and performance optimization" — schema and query-shape decisions are made by the same person who will build the screen the data feeds. This is a different accountability shape than the Backend Engineer entry's lens (ACID/MVCC transactional-correctness reasoning under concurrency), which is about correctness of the store in isolation, not continuity from store to screen.

Deployment/ops fluency sufficient to ship and operate what was built, not just hand it off: ClickHouse requires "automated tests (unit, integration, and E2E) as part of a CI/CD workflow" and Banyan requires specific "Production experience on AWS or Azure" (GCP-only experience explicitly does not satisfy this bar); EarnIn's posting states the same expectation as an explicit full-stack-wide responsibility — "establish and uphold full-stack engineering standards, including patterns, tooling, testing strategies, and CI/CD best practices" — rather than a backend-only concern. (A widely-copied staffing-platform JD template, Toptal's, assigns the same duty and a lower 3-year experience floor for the role in general; it is a generic template, not a live employer posting, so it is cited here only as confirmatory background, not as load-bearing evidence for the senior bar — see the seniority bullet below.)

Seniority bar is scope-based, not just years, and the scope is full-stack-specific in a way a single-discipline mentoring claim is not: EarnIn's senior full-stack posting requires "4+ years of industry experience, with meaningful depth across both frontend and backend development," and scopes the senior hire to "own and evolve both frontend and backend architecture," "mentor and guide engineers across frontend and backend disciplines, raising the bar on code quality, architectural thinking, and delivery practices," and "drive cross-team technical initiatives, aligning frontend and backend architecture with platform capabilities and product strategy" — the mentoring and architecture-ownership scope itself spans both layers in the same sentence, not one layer at a time the way a Backend- or Frontend-only senior mentoring claim would. ClickHouse separately sets a higher floor for a senior title specifically: "5+ years of experience building and shipping production full-stack applications." (Toptal's generic staffing-template JD sets a lower 3-year floor for the role in general; it is included only as an explicitly-hedged, template-derived data point on the low end of the range, not as evidence of the senior-specific, cross-layer scope bar — that claim rests on EarnIn and ClickHouse, both live employer postings.)

The defining trait is named explicitly as breadth-plus-depth, not double-depth, and this is now corroborated by two independent sources rather than one: ClickHouse hires "T-shaped engineers who bring deep frontend expertise while remaining pragmatic and effective beyond UI tasks" — deep in one layer, competent enough to be unblocked everywhere else. Independently, an engineering-management source frames the identical shape as risk mitigation rather than a hiring nicety: "Specialists create bottlenecks. Generalists risk not going deep enough... T-Shaped engineers sidestep both issues, offering the adaptability of a generalist with the mastery of a specialist" (Unosquare) — a different author, a different site, and a different argument (bottleneck/single-point-of-failure avoidance rather than ClickHouse's hiring-bar framing) converging on the same shape.

Valued specifically for velocity and ownership, with the tradeoff against specialization now named in two independent sources rather than one: "full-stack teams excel at delivering features quickly with fewer coordination costs" while "specialised teams develop deeper expertise" (em-tools.io); independently, a second engineering-management source states the same tradeoff from the specialist-risk side: over-reliance on narrow ("I-shaped") specialists means the team is exposed when "the one person who knows how to handle that critical piece of code" is unavailable and "work stalls, the backlog piles up" (Unosquare) — i.e., two independently-authored sources agree the tradeoff runs in the same direction, even though both remain secondary engineering-management commentary rather than primary/official sources; this is flagged, not hidden, in the socratic residual below.

Known failure mode a senior-level interview must probe for, so this isn't just breadth-as-mediocrity, also now dual-sourced: "'Full-stack' often means 'competent at everything, expert at nothing,' and if you do not manage skill development deliberately, quality drifts in every layer" (em-tools.io), echoed independently as "Generalists risk not going deep enough" (Unosquare) — a genuine senior hire must show real depth in at least one layer, not surface familiarity everywhere.
